data.title: Wise: Growth And Innovation From The Inside Out Should Fuel Upside
data.description:
A year has already passed since my initial coverage of Wise plc (WPLCF). And despite my bullish outlook, did not have a significant uptrend. It reached its maximum of $15.80 before momentum weakened and led to a continued downtrend. At its price today, the stock is still a bit far from recovery, and I can't blame the market for maintaining their cautious stance. On the other hand, I do believe that the stock is criminally underappreciated and even misunderstood by many. Although margin growth weakened, its stronger market positioning and robust fundamentals show its potential. Its valuation is also low at this level and shows some upside. Technicals adhere to it as buying opportunities emerge.

Wise plc: How It Has Been Lately

One year after my first analysis, Wise plc stayed strong with its sustained growth and expanding coverage across many countries. This shows its increasing demand and brand popularity due to its strategic cost management, which helps it set competitive pricing to reign over its competitors. And as it kept building products and getting more licenses, its organic growth continued. This was shown in its most recent performance.

Its underlying income, which also served as its operating revenue in Q3 2026, amounted to £424.4M, up by 21.4% YoY from £349.5M. This YoY increase was also much higher than in my previous coverage at 13.4%. This proved that Wise's expansion across multiple countries, accounts, and niches continued to pay off.

This should not be surprising considering its larger customer base. As you can see, its customer base has continuously increased for nine consecutive quarters. It slowed down from Q2 2025 to Q4 2025. But since Q1 2026, Wise has regained its pace. Cross-border volume also had sustained growth on both a YoY and QoQ basis. Lastly, customer balances had 31% YoY growth. It is also worth noting that its QoQ growth trend has been generally upward. This showed its acceleration over the years.

This year, I believe that Wise may sustain its growth through its continued expansion in other niches to cover more financial services. I also believe that there are new opportunities associated with increased market volatility, which I will discuss more in the next section.

Possible Risks

Despite its solid business model and robust performance, I believe that Wise is not entirely shielded from weak external factors. For instance, inflation may raise prices, which can increase its cost pressures. This is important today as oil prices skyrocket. Why? Oil/fuel affects multiple industries, especially large and asset-heavy ones like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial. Considering their important roles and impact on other industries, the negative spillovers may extend into various sectors. This may even push inflation more. This may affect its pricing/rate flexibility.

Of course, it must also think about the competition with large peers PayPal (PYPL) and Revolut. The consolation is that its business model and expansion strategies make it unique, limiting its exposure to competition.

Core Strengths And Drivers

We all know that Wise is not exactly foolproof since it builds its own infrastructure from scratch. It does not capitalize on M&As for expansion. Instead, it concentrates on penetrating and adding new countries to its services and building products continuously, like Wise Account and Wise Business. This makes its expansion and global market coverage smaller than PayPal.

On the other hand, I believe that this is strategic since Wise aims to maintain its ultra-low-cost business model. That way, it can keep its prices low to attract more customers and generate more transactions. Also, it does not have to deal with complex integration processes and costs, which may break its pricing advantages. In short, Wise builds everything in-house and expands geographically and technologically instead of buying other companies. As such, Wise remains a clean and focused business. Although expansion is lower with fewer step-change growth drivers, its decision helps it maintain strong cost discipline and control over its tech stack to preserve its margins.

Another potential core strength and driver is its Wise Travel card. This multi-currency debit card allows users to hold, convert, and spend money in over 100 countries and various currencies. It's like holding multiple wallets in a single app. And with its cheap currency conversion using the mid-market or real exchange rate, this may become more attractive to customers. Imagine a traveler avoiding the hassle of going to money changers to have their currencies converted or exchanged into another. You can simply pay in local/foreign stores and withdraw from local/foreign ATMs with its auto-convert feature. So, it's not surprising to see 75,000 customers joining its waiting list in one month in India alone. Note that India is among the top 10 countries with the most number of freelancers. More importantly, India is a rapidly growing market in tourism. As of 2025, they are already the fifth largest number of travelers with outbound destinations, including Europe, Australia, the Middle East, the US, and Southeast Asia. With that, the Wise Travel card may become a staple for them.

I also like that it focuses on countries with many freelancers, including the Philippines, because cross-border payment apps are crucial in cheap and real-time payments and currency conversion. Wise and PYPL are also much cheaper than domestic banks here. And because most clients come from the US/CAN, EU, and Australia, Wise plays an integral part in the day-to-day operations of many businesses without a physical presence in countries where their employees are located. This may be supported by the stronger USD against many other currencies. The extreme volatility in the global oil market can be detrimental to many businesses. But Wise may benefit from it. After all, a stronger USD and GBP mean more PHP after conversion. This can increase cross-border transaction volumes and conversions. And I think businesses will keep hiring offshore employees to keep their labor costs low or manageable amid stubborn inflation.

Wise may also benefit from the current trend because the skyrocketing oil prices may affect multiple industries, especially large and asset-heavy ones like shipping, logistics, industrial, and manufacturing. With that, inflation may become more stubborn, and central banks may pause their interest rate cuts or even increase interest rates. As a result, Wise may derive more interest income from customer balances it stores in banks and liquid government securities.

Lastly, Wise is a very liquid company. If you look at its most recent Balance Sheet, cash and investments comprise 96% of its assets. Their combined amount is more than enough to cover all liabilities. It can also cover all its borrowings in a single payment even if it doesn't have to. More interestingly, its borrowings have a long maturity profile because 98% of the total has a maturity of more than one year. This proves that Wise has enough reserves to sustain its operating capacity.

Valuation

With price ratios, I was able to update its valuation. Its P/E shows that Wise trades at 24.48x its EPS. While this can be considered high, I believe that this is reasonable if we look at its PEG. If we look at its four-year CAGR of 29%, the PEG is 0.84x (24.48/28.99). This means that WPLCF is still a bit cheap at this level. Also, its present ratio is much lower than the average of 42.78x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $21.39.

We can also disregard 2022 in the computation because it appears to be an outlier in the group. If we focus on 2023-2025, the average will only be 30.90x. Even so, this will lead to a higher TP of $15.45. The same applies to P/B, which gives a TP of $21.37. This is almost identical to the P/E TP, which proves their consistency in valuing the stock. If we exclude 2022 again, the average P/B will be 8.12x. But this will still lead to a higher TP of $15.26. Hence, the stock is still reasonably cheap today.

Technicals

WPLCF's technicals are still weak as the price trend grapples with recovery. But we can also see that the price trend is now moving in line with the 50-day SMA, showing that the current momentum is already catching up with the average in the past 50 days. The 50-day SMA has already flattened after the sharp downtrend, which means that market sentiments have already neutralized and become less pessimistic. This can imply that risks have already been priced in at this level.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show new buying opportunities. The MACD line is increasing again above the signal line as buying volume rebounds. The histogram is now within positive territory, showing that buying volume is starting to outpace selling volume. Lastly, RSI is increasing, which shows some improvements in momentum. There is also enough buying room, so I believe that the current price is an ideal entry point.

Conclusion

Wise plc is still a robust company as it tries to expand its market coverage and niches without having to go head-to-head completely with large peers like PayPal. Its effective pricing and cost management strategies and solid liquidity ensure that it's well-positioned despite market volatility. Its valuation is also still reasonably cheap and suggests some upside potential. Technicals are still lagging, but new buying opportunities and improving sentiments adhere to fundamentals. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NXG Rights Offering Quick Notes
data.description:
NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund's (NXG) offering is live. The ex-rights date was April 6, 2026, while the offering is due to expire on April 30, 2026.

This is a transferable 1-for-3 rights offering that uses a more typical CEF formula: the higher of 95% of the average closing market price on the final 5 days of the offering or 92% of NAV. This structure effectively caps the subscription discount to market at 8%, which limits maximum dilution. The NAV floor is activated once the fund's market price is at roughly a -3.2% discount to NAV.

From the press release:

March 26, 2026 | NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NYSE: NXG) Announces Terms of Rights Offering. The board of trustees (the "Board") of NXG NextGen Infrastructure Income Fund (NXG) (the "Fund") has approved the terms of the issuance of transferable rights ("Rights") to the holders of the Fund's common shares (the "Common Shareholders") of beneficial interest, par value $0.001 per share ("Common Shares"), as of the record date, entitling the holders of those Rights to subscribe for Common Shares (the "Offer"). The record date for the Offer is currently expected to be April 6, 2026 (the "Record Date"). The Fund will distribute to Common Shareholders on the Record Date ("Record Date Common Shareholders") one Right for each Common Share held on the Record Date. Common Shareholders will be entitled to purchase one new Common Share for every three Rights held (1 for 3). The proposed subscription period will commence on the Record Date and is currently anticipated to expire on April 30, 2026, unless extended by the Fund (the "Expiration Date"). Rights may be exercised at any time during the subscription period. The Rights are transferable and are expected to be admitted for trading on the New York Stock Exchange LLC (the "NYSE") under the symbol "NXG RT" during the course of the Offer. The subscription price per Common Share (the "Subscription Price") will be determined on the Expiration Date, and will be equal to 95% of the average of the last reported sales price of a Common Share of the Fund on the NYSE on the Expiration Date and each of the four (4) immediately preceding trading days (the "Formula Price"). If, however, the Formula Price is less than 92% of the Fund's net asset value per Common Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on the Expiration Date, the Subscription Price will be 92% of the Fund's net asset value per Common Share at the close of trading on the NYSE on that day.

As can be seen from the chart below, NXG's valuation had peaked into premium territory during March, which was likely the trigger for management to initiate the rights offering. As is typical, however, the announcement itself provides a catalyst for sentiment reversal, as prospective dilution and the prospect of new shares being issued below the current market price result in a widening of the discount. The current discount is -4.51%, and we may still expect this to move lower towards the discount floor of -8% as the expiry date approaches.

Unlike previous BlackRock and Nuveen rights offerings we discussed where the managers paid for the offering expenses, NXG's rights offering will carry a sales load of 3.50% that will be borne by shareholders. This will exert an additional dilutive effect on the NAV/share for all shareholders, regardless of whether they subscribe.

So what should investors do now? For investors holding rights and who wish to expand their position in NXG, they could consider subscribing if the discount is narrower than -8%, as this would allow them to receive discounted shares versus the market price. However, if the discount widens beyond -8% at expiry, then it would be preferable to not subscribe and buy shares on the open market instead.

For those investors who do not wish to expand their share count in NXG, they should sell their rights on the open market to compensate for the impending dilution. Historically, the rights value has tended to decay as expiration approaches; therefore, selling the rights sooner rather than later may provide the best outcome.

The rights are currently showing at a price of 18.5 cents on MarketWatch, although I doubt that they are actively traded yet.

Summary

In summary, this is a basic action plan for NXG investors:

  1. Subscribe if:
    • The discount remains narrower than -8% at expiry.
    • You want to increase your position and believe in NXG long-term.
  2. Do NOT subscribe if:
    • The discount widens beyond -8% at expiry.
    • It would be cheaper to buy shares directly on the open market instead.
  3. If you don't want to subscribe:
    • Sell your rights on the open market, as the value of the rights may potentially decline as the offering period progresses.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SVOL: Managed Volatility Exposure With An Appealing Twist
data.description:
The Simplify Volatility Premium ETF (SVOL) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with income through inverse exposure to the Cboe Market Volatility Index (VIX) while protecting returns through the use of a call options strategy.

SVOL can be best utilized following periods of increased volatility like the present market environment. While timing volatility increases can be challenging given the exogenous effects on the market, particularly in today’s high-risk geopolitical environment, taking a short position in volatility may achieve net returns under the assumption that volatility typically remediates down to an average rate. Given the heightened levels of volatility and the earnings potential for SVOL, I recommend SVOL with a "Buy" rating. As a result of the futures derivatives used to capture exposure, SVOL can only target daily exposure in terms of its target performance profile; holding SVOL longer than a single trading day may provide deviation from the -0.2x to -0.3x target range and may compound over time.

Investment Rationale for SVOL

SVOL was designed to capture inverse exposure to VIX while offering protection from losses through the use of a strategic options overlay, limiting total losses by the fund. The strategy was designed to provide -0.2x to -0.3x the performance of short-term VIX futures, which may reduce the volatility of the price of the actual fund, providing a certain degree of stability for investors.

Volatility has become a growing risk in recent weeks following the start of the war in Iran. The S&P 500 Index (SPX) has returned roughly -4% since the start of the military escalation of the war on February 28, 2026, exhibiting days of sharp volatility throughout. While the market has made attempts to recover on signs of abating oil prices or an end to combat, positive news has been relatively short-lived, with the market falling back into decline.

As a result of market tension, volatility has experienced substantial spikes throughout the last month, presenting short-term opportunities for investors to capture incremental downside risk, whether using short-term leveraged strategies like the ProShares Short VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (SVXY), which can capture -0.5x the daily inverse performance of VIX, or SVOL, which provides -0.2x to 0.3x the inverse performance of VIX. Depending on the investor’s risk appetite, each portfolio can present value during a period of heightened volatility, underpinned by the assumption that VIX remediates to a lower, steady-state range following sharp spikes.

By comparison, SVOL can be appealing to investors given the structure of the fund, capturing the remediation component of VIX while protecting from downside risk when VIX sporadically spikes. As protection for the fund’s net assets, SVOL may allocate upwards of 20% of the assets to an options overlay to mitigate heightened volatility risk. To do this, SVOL purchases put and call options on the VIX or a VIX-linked ETP to hedge against adverse movements. Strategically, SVOL will then sell call options when VIX spikes and sell puts to capture the return to normalcy.

Through the active, deep out-of-the-money options trading strategy, SVOL may provide investors with greater income opportunities beyond the futures contracts. This can become more appealing during periods of greater swings in volatility, as volatility [denoted as Vega] inherently increases the premium for options contracts. In general, SVOL may provide investors with a certain degree of stability throughout periods of greater volatility, capturing both the upside & downside risk through the use of its options overlay strategy along with an assortment of other security exposures that can offset VIX exposure.

Accordingly, SVOL holds an assortment of Simplify bond, equity, and managed derivatives funds to enhance the strategy, capturing income across multiple verticals.

Over the last 12 months, SVOL has paid out $3.55/share in distributions, yielding 22.90%. Distributions are paid out monthly. Under the notion that SVOL can provide limited risk exposure to VIX, the fund may be an appealing investment strategy for those seeking alternatives to traditional fixed income funds. Given its cross-asset exposure, SVOL can be said to provide diversified exposure across the financial markets with an emphasis on volatility risk management.

While shares will exhibit compounded risk and NAV decay over time, I believe the upside exposure may outweigh downside risk over time, especially if volatility were to abate in due time.

About Simplify Volatility Premium ETF

SVOL was launched by Simplify ETFs on May 12, 2021, on the NYSE Arca Exchange and has an expense ratio of 66bps. The fund has a relatively active market with $590 million in net assets, with an average of $9.94 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

Risks Related to SVOL

SVOL is a structured strategy designed to capture the daily inverse performance of VIX, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered before making a final investment decision. Trading VIX can be considered speculative given that it holds no fundamental value for pricing. The strategy may expose investors to various exogenous factors over time, including macroeconomic risks like inflation, interest rates, jobs market reports, and GDP growth, among others. SVOL may also expose investors to geopolitical risks like war and international trade relations, as well as other factors that may influence the direction of the market.

SVOL may expose investors to compounded returns rather than rolling returns, which may amplify performance when held for longer than a single trading day. SVOL employs an options overlay strategy that may only perform during periods of heightened volatility risk. SVOL may experience NAV decay over time resulting from its structured trading strategy.

Final Thoughts

SVOL can be an appealing investment strategy during a turbulent market environment, underpinned by the assumption that volatility reverts to a mean rate. While volatility may remain heightened in today’s geopolitical environment, I believe that it may abate in due time as the market normalizes, potentially providing investors with an appealing entry to capture upside risk. Given the appealing structure of the fund and income component, I am recommending SVOL with a "Buy" rating.

data.title: Capital City Bank Group: Loan Declines And Valuation Concerns Lead To My Downgrade
data.description:
This article serves as an update to my previous analysis of Capital City Bank Group (CCBG). I first wrote about the company on October 27 of last year, calling its stock a buy. My opinion was based on a number of factors, including the company's track record of strong performance, reasonable valuation at that time, and dividend growth. Since that article was published, CCBG has produced a total return of just over 11%. The State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF has gained only 9.2% during this same time period, while the S&P 500 has lost 4.25%, as shown in the graph below.

With Capital City's first earnings report for 2026 less than three weeks away, I thought it was time to revisit my earlier analysis and see what, if anything, has changed in the company's performance. The share price for CCBG has held up quite well this year, especially during the last two months when most of its peers have pulled back. However, there are a few areas where the company has started trending downward since my last article was published. Loan growth for its banking operations has been absent now for two straight years, and a few other performance ratios took a step back in the last quarter of 2025. All of these concerns will be discussed in more detail in this analysis, as I move my assessment of CCBG to a Hold.

Company Overview

Capital City Bank Group is the holding company for Capital City Bank, named because its founding is tied to Tallahassee, the capital of Florida. With a history that dates back to 1895, Capital City is considered one of the oldest banking institutions in the US. Today, the bank has 70 branches, with 53 of those in its home state. An additional 15 are located in Georgia and two in Alabama, right on the border with the Peach State.

As I mentioned in my first analysis, Capital City specializes in operating in smaller, less densely populated areas. Only two of its Georgia branches are in Metro Atlanta, and none are in the city itself. The majority of its branches in Florida are in smaller towns with names like Chipley, Hastings, and Spring Hill. A complete map of the bank's locations with some additional information about Capital City Bank Group can be seen below.

CCBG currently has a market cap of just over $758 million. At the end of FY 2025, the company reported total assets of $4.4 billion, which included a loan portfolio valued at $2.5 billion. A breakdown of the types of loans held for investment by CCBG is given in the graphs below.

Residential mortgages take up the largest share of the bank's loan mix at 40.3%. If the 9.5% allocated to home equity loans is added to this amount, then the total exposure to residential real estate is approaching 50%. The next largest weight in the portfolio belongs to commercial real estate at 30.2%, with 30% of that CRE connected to retail properties. No other category has a double-digit allocation in the bank's held-for-investment loan book, but I will point out that roughly 6% of the bank's loans are indirect auto loans. Those who have read my previous analyses of other regional banks know that this is one of my least favorite loan types because of their high default rates.

At the end of last year, Capital City held total deposits of $3.7 billion, which was basically flat from where things stood at the end of 2024. However, one of the strengths of the company is its large portion of deposits that are in non-interest-bearing accounts. On December 31, 2025, those accounts were valued at $1.3 billion. This helps push the bank's cost of deposits down to just 0.82%, 130 basis points below its peers.

CCBG has a forward dividend yield of 2.44%. The company has an 11-year streak of increased annual payouts that already appears to be poised to move to 12. Capital City Bank Group announced at the end of February that its Q1 2026 dividend would get a 4% raise from Q4 2025 to $0.27. This marked the second time in the last three quarters that the company has boosted its quarterly distribution. Yet, its payout rate is still reasonable at 30%.

What I Like About Capital City Bank Group

I will start off this analysis discussing the things that I still like about Capital City Bank Group. First is one of the most important, earnings growth. CCBG reported diluted earnings per share of $3.60 in FY 2025, a 15.4% improvement from FY 2024. When I last wrote about the bank, last year's EPS was projected to be just $3.49. Capital City's bottom-line success can be attributed to a 7.9% jump in net interest income and an 8.4% improvement in non-interest income. Non-interest expenses only moved up 1.0% in the last 12 months, which helped the bank improve its efficiency ratio by over 4.5% to 65.71%.

Unlike other small-cap community banks that are more dependent on interest income, CCBG's fee income was able to comprise 32.4% of the bank's operating revenues in 2025. Wealth management fees and mortgage banking fees are significant drivers of this diversified revenue flow. Wealth management fees have increased 26.5% since FY 2023. Mortgage banking fees have risen 63.1% over the last 24 months. At the end of last year, total assets under management totaled $2.9 billion, a slight decline from the $3.0 billion at the end of 2024. However, that decrease was attributed to lower retail brokerage assets caused by the sale of a company subsidiary, Capital City Strategic Wealth.

Asset quality at the bank also appears to be in good shape. Nonperforming loans in FY 2025 did rise 10 basis points over the last 12 months, but it was still just 0.34%, a low ratio by most standards. Classified loans dropped from 0.75% at the end of FY 2024 to just 0.56% at the end of last year. Net charge-offs also declined year-over-year to 0.14%.

What Are My Concerns for Capital City Bank Group

My list of concerns for the company is certainly longer than it was just a few months ago. The bank's total loans have now declined for two straight years, and the pace of the decrease is speeding up. After ending 2023 at $2.7 billion, these assets declined 3% in FY 2024 and an additional 4% in FY 2025. There can be any number of reasons for this, and not all of them would be considered negative. For example, last year the bank decreased its exposure to construction loans and auto loans. I am certainly not one to be disappointed in a decline in auto loans, but the end result of two years of loan book shrinkage means it will be increasingly challenging for the bank to grow its net interest income and ultimately its overall earnings in the year ahead.

Three of the key performance metrics that I follow for regional banks all declined in Q4 2025 when compared to the previous quarter. Return on assets dropped 22 basis points to 1.25%. The fall in return on equity was much more noticeable as it lost almost two full percentage points, from 11.67% at the end of Q3 to just 9.78% at the end of Q4. Net interest margin slipped to 4.26% from 4.34% over the last three months. For the full year, all of these three data points - ROA, ROE, and NIM - did improve from FY 2024, so this is something to watch more closely when the results from Q1 2026 are released.

My last concern comes from the bank's valuation. CCBG now has a forward PE of 12.69. In 2025, CCBG's stock price rose 19.5%. During that same 12-month period, its tangible book value rose 14.3%, from $23.65 to $27.03. The stock currently trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.64, which is higher than the national average that stood at about 1.44 at the end of last year. These numbers make me less enthusiastic about the stock's performance for the first half of this year when compared to its similar-sized peers.

Additional Risks to Consider

There are a couple of additional risks to consider before making an investment in Capital City Bank Group. First is the bank's heavy dependence on the state of Florida for its growth and future profit opportunities. While the Sunshine State has certainly enjoyed strong growth in recent years, it appears to be cooling off in several areas. Real GDP is expected to rise just 1.4% in Q1 2026 after seeing a 3.3% gain as recently as Q2 2025. Q2 2026 GDP is not predicted to be much better, with a gain of 1.6%. Job growth, which was negative in the second half of 2025, is supposed to show losses again in Q1 2026 before turning around in Q2. Home prices are predicted to drop 3.5% in Q1 2026 and 2.2% in Q2 2026 before correcting in the second half of the year. Higher insurance premiums have been blamed for a surge in existing homes on the market. A sluggish economy could make CCBG's profit growth in the current year more challenging and could also put pressure on nonperforming loan ratios.

Even if Florida's economic activity surprises investors to the upside, there is an increasing chance that the overall US economy will see a slowdown in Q2 that may stretch into Q3 of this year. Higher fuel prices, sagging consumer confidence, and higher interest rates could all play a role here. The odds of a recession in the US have increased significantly since the start of the year, which has made me more selective in the banks that I recommend on this site.

The Bottom Line

I still consider Capital City Bank Group to be a solid regional bank, but it does not stand out in the same way that it did just a little over five months ago. This year may be more challenging for all small-cap banks, as the interest rate cuts that were expected at the start of the year appear much less likely to materialize, at least in the first half of 2026. The company's valuation, both from the perspective of profit and tangible book value, appears a bit elevated at the current time, leading me to my decision to downgrade CCBG to a Hold.

data.title: MOO: Investing Across The Global Food Supply Chain
data.description:
VanEck Agribusiness ETF (MOO) tracks the global agribusiness value chain, from fertilizers and farm equipment to food processing and distribution, and the companies shaping the future of food.

Feeding the world is increasingly viewed as a significant economic and societal challenge.

A growing global population, shifting diets and increasing climate pressures are forcing agricultural systems to produce more with fewer resources and greater efficiency. At the same time, supply chain disruptions and geopolitical tensions have exposed just how fragile those systems can be.

For investors, this is not only a macroeconomic challenge. It may also represent an evolving opportunity across the global food supply chain and the broader agribusiness sector.

What is Agribusiness?

Agribusiness refers to the network of companies involved in producing, processing and distributing food and agricultural products. This includes everything from fertilizers, seeds and farm equipment to food processing, trading and distribution.

Rather than focusing solely on farming or commodities, agribusiness encompasses the full value chain that brings food from field to consumer.

The VanEck Agribusiness ETF offers comprehensive exposure to the agribusiness industry by seeking to replicate the MVIS® Global Agribusiness Index (MVMOOTR). The index comprises a globally diversified group of agribusiness companies, including those engaged in agri-chemicals, animal health and fertilizers, seeds and traits, irrigation equipment and farm machinery, aquaculture and fishing, livestock, cultivation and plantations, and trading of agricultural products.

MOO targets businesses positioned within key areas of modern food systems. In these areas, factors such as scarcity, geopolitics and climate volatility may influence investment and, in some cases, pricing dynamics.

How Agribusiness Investing Benefits from Market Disruptions

Recent years have underscored how vulnerable global food systems can be. Pandemic-related shutdowns, geopolitical tensions and climate-related disruptions have strained supply chains, from fertilizer shortages to transportation bottlenecks.

At the same time, food inflation has remained a key concern for both consumers and policymakers.

While these dynamics can present challenges, they may also reinforce the importance of agribusiness companies. Food demand is inherently inelastic, meaning consumption remains relatively stable across economic cycles. At the same time, many firms have the ability to pass higher input costs through the value chain over time. Supply disruptions can also tighten availability and support pricing for producers and processors.

In this context, agribusiness exposure may serve as a potential buffer during inflationary or supply-constrained environments, although outcomes may vary.

Investing Across the Agriculture Value Chain

These structural pressures do not impact agriculture uniformly. Instead, they flow through different parts of the value chain in distinct ways.

To understand where these opportunities emerge, it helps to step back and look at the full agribusiness ecosystem:

Each segment of the value chain responds differently to the same underlying pressures. This creates distinct drivers of growth, risk and return. MOO captures this full ecosystem, offering diversified exposure across three key segments:

1. Agricultural Inputs (Upstream)

These companies provide the essential farming building blocks:

  • Fertilizers & agricultural chemicals (e.g., Nutrien Ltd. (NTR), Mosaic Co. (MOS))
  • Seeds & crop protection (e.g., Corteva, Inc. (CTVA))
  • Animal health (e.g., Zoetis, Inc. (ZTS))

These businesses play an important role in improving crop yields and efficiency as arable land becomes more constrained.

Animal health, which is less directly tied to commodity cycles, has been a relatively resilient contributor within the portfolio. This may reflect the defensive characteristics of certain subsegments.

2. Equipment & Infrastructure (Midstream)

Mechanization and logistics are central to modern agriculture:

  • Farm machinery (e.g., Deere & Co. (DE), CNH Industrial NV (CNH), Kubota Corp. (KUBTF)(KUBTY))
  • Transportation and storage (e.g., rail and grain logistics)

These firms may benefit from long-term trends such as precision agriculture and farm automation while remaining sensitive to farm income cycles.

3. Processing, Trading & Food Production (Downstream)

This segment connects farms to consumers:

  • Grain traders and processors (e.g., Bunge Global SA (BG))
  • Protein producers and packaged food companies (e.g., Tyson Foods Inc. (TSN), Mowi ASA (MNHVF)(MHGVY))

These companies play an important role in managing global supply chains by sourcing, storing and distributing food where it is needed most. During periods of volatility, their scale and network advantages may become more relevant.

Portfolio Positioning at a Glance

Understanding how MOO is constructed helps reinforce how it captures the agribusiness opportunity in practice.

Top Holdings

Ticker Name Weight (%)
DE Deere & Company 8.94
ZTS Zoetis, Inc. Class A 7.39
506921 Bayer AG 6.78
CTVA Corteva Inc. 6.66
NTR Nutrien Ltd. 6.35
ADM Archer-Daniels-Midland Company 5.43
TSN Tyson Foods, Inc. Class A 4.69
6326 Kubota Corporation 4.47
CF CF Industries Holdings, Inc. 4.16
BG Bunge Global SA 3.59

Source: VanEck. Data as of 2/28/2026. This is not an offer to buy or sell, or recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities mentioned herein. Holdings are subject to change.

A snapshot of MOO’s top holdings highlights exposure to leading global agribusiness companies across inputs, equipment and food production.

Takeaway: The fund is concentrated in established, globally recognized agribusiness companies that play important roles across the food supply chain.

Agricultural Supply Chain Weights Exposure

MOO’s exposure spans key segments of the agricultural supply chain, reflecting the same value chain framework illustrated earlier. Based on the chart, allocations include approximately 25% to seeds, fertilizers and agricultural chemicals, 21% to farm equipment and machinery, 20% to livestock, aquaculture and fishing, 17% to animal health, 10% to agricultural trading and 6% to cultivation and farming.

Takeaway: The portfolio is diversified across multiple parts of the agribusiness value chain, with meaningful exposure to both inputs and downstream activities. This balance may help capture different drivers of performance across the global food supply chain.

Geographic Exposure

The portfolio spans both developed and emerging markets, providing global exposure to companies operating across the agricultural value chain.

Takeaway: This global footprint reflects the international nature of food production and distribution while providing diversified exposure across regions.

A Distinct Sector Profile

MOO’s exposure reflects the real economy of food production:

  • Heavy allocations to consumer staples, materials and industrials
  • Minimal exposure to technology-heavy sectors that dominate broad indices

This differentiation means MOO may behave differently than traditional equity portfolios and may offer diversification benefits, particularly during periods of inflation or market stress.

Key Trends in Agriculture and Agribusiness Investing

Structural pressures are accelerating innovation across the system. Precision agriculture and automation are improving yields and reducing costs, while biological inputs and sustainable solutions are gaining traction amid environmental concerns. Companies are also investing in supply chain resilience, and innovation in protein production, including aquaculture, is expanding. These developments are reshaping the landscape for agriculture investing.

How Food Inflation and Supply Chains Impact Agribusiness

Inflation and supply disruptions can create short-term volatility, but they also reinforce long-term investment themes. Food security has become a priority for governments and corporations, while higher baseline prices can support revenues across the value chain. At the same time, innovation is accelerating as producers seek to improve efficiency and offset rising costs.

Agribusiness companies are not just exposed to these dynamics. They are also part of the solution.

What Drives Agribusiness ETF Performance

Agribusiness sits at the intersection of cyclical and structural forces. In the short term, performance is influenced by:

  • Commodity prices
  • Weather patterns
  • Input costs

Over the long term, key drivers include:

  • Population growth
  • Dietary shifts
  • Productivity demands

This cyclical nature can create entry points for long-term investors.

Why Invest in the VanEck Agribusiness ETF?

  • Global exposure across developed and emerging markets
  • Pure-play agribusiness focus
  • Diversified value chain exposure
  • Structural growth tied to food demand and innovation
  • Exposure to a non-discretionary industry
  • Potential diversification benefits

The Bottom Line

Agriculture is evolving rapidly, driven by technology, sustainability and global demand. The VanEck Agribusiness ETF provides a way to participate in this transformation through the companies enabling the future of food.

Important Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. MVIS® Global Agribusiness Index (MVMOOTR) is intended to track the overall performance of companies involved in agri-chemicals, animal health and fertilizers, seeds and traits, from farm/irrigation equipment and farm machinery, aquaculture and fishing, livestock, cultivation and plantations (including grain, oil palms, sugar cane, tobacco leaves, grapevines, etc.), and trading of agricultural products.

MVIS Global Agribusiness Index is the exclusive property of MarketVector Indexes GmbH (a wholly owned subsidiary of the Adviser), which has contracted with Solactive AG to maintain and calculate the Index. data.title: Micron: The Only Chip Stock I'm Buying
data.description:
Amid this year's flight to safety in the stock markets, the safest bet in the tech sector has emerged as chip stocks. After all, most chip manufacturers (especially memory companies) are sold out, thanks to the data center boom.

That guarantee of solid revenue growth throughout the next few years has caused a skyrocketing in the semiconductor industry, leaving most companies to be incredibly overvalued. To me, there is one exception: Micron (MU), who, alongside Samsung, SK Hynix, and Western Digital (WDC), are the world's suppliers of NAND memory. Shares of Micron have leaped ~5x over the past year, but the stock is down ~20% since reporting Q2 results in mid-March. In my view, the market's recent profit-taking is a great time to add this stock on recent weakness.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Micron in January, when the stock was trading in the $340s. Since then, Micron briefly spiked to above $460 and has quickly ratcheted down since the company's mid-March earnings release, which I view to be a "buy the rumor, sell the news" pattern on very strong fiscal Q2 (February quarter) results. With an opportunity to buy Micron on this breather, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Strong Near-Term Demand Coupled With Longer-Term Contracts

It's impossible to discuss Micron without immediately addressing the company's sharp revenue growth rates, which underscore how rampant demand is for its memory chips. The chart below showcases Micron's revenue by business unit:

Overall, Micron's total revenue of $23.86 billion increased by a whopping 196% y/y, or tripled y/y. Even though supply is constrained (and as such, Micron itself should have high visibility into what its near-term revenue should be), Micron beat Wall Street's revenue expectations of $19.76 billion (+145% y/y) by a wide margin.

What we are most interested in dissecting, however, is the fact that Micron's demand isn't all lopsided. Yes, it's true that Micron notes that demand from data centers is expected to reach 50% of industry TAM in 2026. But this isn't the entire story for Micron. Though a smaller slice of overall company revenue, we note that memory sales to mobile and client (PC) devices rose 245% y/y, while sales to the automotive industry also grew 162% y/y. Memory supply constraints are turning all end markets into voracious buyers and giving Micron the benefit of incredibly elevated pricing (more details in the next section).

For calendar 2026, as shown in the chart below, Micron still expects both DRAM and NAND chips to be fully supply-constrained, with conditions "tight" well past 2026. Importantly, Micron notes that it's now expecting DRAM demand to rise in the low 20s this year, slightly higher than "around 20%" in the prior outlook.

The key worry that many investors have about Micron is this: What if we're investing in Micron at the very peak of its demand cycle? Note that in the past, the memory chip industry has been very boom-and-bust-like airline stocks, with suppliers like Micron and Western Digital ratcheting up capacity during boom times and being forced to cut prices and margins when demand slows down.

What has changed in this cycle, however, is the fact that Micron is now seeking much longer customer commitments. In Q2, the company kicked off what it calls "strategic customer agreements." These SCAs are more specific multi-year commitments, and the company notes that in Q2, it signed its first five-year SCA.

This provides Micron critical visibility that there will be demand to absorb supply much further out than the current supply-constrained 2026-2027 window. This is critical as the company has invested heavily into capacity that won't yield supply until 2028. Earlier this year in January, the company acquired 300,000 square feet of manufacturing capacity in Tongluo, China, which is expected to deliver shipments at scale in 2028. Similarly, recent investments into manufacturing in Singapore are expected to deliver HBM supply in calendar 2027 and new NAND supply in 2028.

Micron's new long-term SCAs indicate that the company isn't just guessing at demand in 2028 and beyond to purchase chips from its long-term expansion sites. In short, we applaud Micron's fierce ~3x y/y revenue growth today, but even more importantly, the company is signing on business for the years ahead as well.

Pricing and Margins Are Unlocking Huge Earnings Growth

The other massive tailwind in Micron's favor is that very tight supply conditions in memory have led to a massive bidding-up of prices. As shown in the chart below, in Q2 Micron benefited from a "mid-60%" sequential increase in DRAM pricing, while NAND increased at an even faster pace in the high 70s.

These are massive pricing jumps in a component that many investors had once thought to be a pure commodity. Micron's gross margin in Q2 hit 74.9%, which improved nearly 20 points sequentially versus 56.0% in Q1 and even far exceeded the company's guidance for just a 68.0% gross margin for the quarter (which indicates that Micron benefited from much richer price appreciation in memory than it had been banking on).

The company expects strength to continue well through Q3, where guidance is calling for gross margins to improve six points sequentially to "approximately 81%." Its revenue guidance for Q3 at $33.5 billion (with a ~2% spread on the high and low end of that midpoint), meanwhile, implies an incredible ~260% y/y growth rate - accelerating from Q2.

Micron's EPS in Q2 hit $12.20, growing ~8x y/y and substantially beating Wall Street's expectations of $9.16 with 33% upside.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

Despite its myriad strengths, Micron remains one of the best-value chip stocks. A surge in forward earnings expectations has positioned Micron's stock at a mere 7x forward P/E. Compare this to direct storage/memory peers like Western Digital and Seagate (STX), which are trading above >30x - at a premium even to market darling NVIDIA (NVDA).

In short, Micron still has plenty of room from a valuation perspective to rise further, especially given the strength of its recent results plus the comfort that we have from multi-year SCA agreements ensuring a healthy demand pipeline over a long timeframe. Stay long here and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Dorchester Minerals: Strong Recent Growth In Oil Sales Volumes
data.description:
Dorchester Minerals (DMLP) reported a second consecutive quarter with a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes. This (along with lease bonus income) helped Dorchester increase its distribution by 10% in Q4 2025 despite relatively weak oil prices.

Since I last looked at Dorchester in December 2025, the oil price environment has changed considerably. At an average of $80 WTI oil in 2026 (and with a recent settlement), Dorchester's average quarterly distribution for 2026 may reach around $0.96 per unit. As well, Q2 2026's distribution should end up well over $1 per unit.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to several factors. These include strong near-term oil prices, an increase in modeled oil sales volumes, and an increase in my long-term WTI oil price from $70 to $75.

Q4 2025 Distribution and Sales Volumes

Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution increased by approximately 10% quarter-over-quarter to $0.755712 per common unit. This increase came despite softening oil prices during the last few months of 2025. Dorchester's Q4 2025 distribution was also boosted by $6.0 million in lease bonus and other income, with over 50% of its full-year lease bonus and other income being recorded in Q4.

As well, Dorchester reported a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in oil sales volumes (after a 15% quarter-over-quarter increase in Q3 2025). Those increased volumes helped offset the impact of the 7% decline in Dorchester's realized oil price between Q3 2025 and Q4 2025.

Dorchester's Sales Volumes Q2 2024 Q3 2024 Q4 2024 Q1 2025 Q2 2025 Q3 2025 Q4 2025
Royalty natural gas sales (mmcf) 1,275 1,569 1,568 1,482 1,341 1,607 1,702
Royalty oil sales (mbbls) 423 642 535 518 399 493 592
NPI natural gas sales (mmcf) 493 642 510 436 493 581 453
NPI oil sales (mbbls) 131 198 140 136 163 160 162

Dorchester's Colorado acquisition (which closed in late August 2025) helped its Q4 oil sales volumes, but it also appears that there was significant quarter-over-quarter organic growth.

Recent Settlement

Dorchester announced that it had entered into a settlement with unrelated third parties to resolve litigation. This litigation involved Midland County leaseholds owned by Dorchester, and the settlement resulted in Dorchester receiving $15.5 million in proceeds that will be included in the calculation of the April 2026 Net Profits Interest payment.

This is a one-time boost that should increase Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution by approximately 31 to 32 cents per unit.

Outlook at $80 WTI Oil in 2026

I had previously modeled Dorchester's 2026 results based on 11,325 BOEPD (64% oil) in sales volumes. That was slightly above its Q3 2025 sales volumes.

Dorchester's Q4 2025 sales volumes jumped to around 12,100 BOEPD (68% oil), including a full quarter of contributions from its Colorado acquisition. Dorchester's sales volumes can vary significantly from quarter to quarter, but I am now modeling Dorchester's 2026 sales volumes at 11,750 BOEPD (66% oil).

At an average WTI oil price of $80, Dorchester may generate $204 million in 2026 revenues. Oil strip prices are quite volatile, but 2026 strip prices have ranged from the mid-$70s to mid-$80s recently.

For the purposes of this calculation, I've excluded the settlement mentioned above.

Revenue $ Million
Royalties $168
Net Profits Interest $24
Other (Including Lease Bonus Income) $12
Total Revenues $204

Dorchester would then be projected to generate $171 million in 2026 distributable cash flow.

$ Million
Operating Expenses $20
G&A $13
Total Cash Expenses $33

Thus, I now expect Dorchester to average around $0.88 to $0.89 per unit in quarterly distributions related to 2026. This excludes the $0.31 to $0.32 per unit in settlement payments, which would increase the quarterly average to around $0.96 per unit.

Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution seems likely to be fairly large. The Q2 cash receipts attributable to its royalty properties would mainly reflect oil sales from March 2026 to May 2026, a period where WTI oil may average $90+ (based on current strip). The Q2 2026 distribution would also benefit from the settlement payment, so it seems likely to be above $1.10 per unit and potentially approaching $1.30 per unit.

Notes on Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 per unit to $34.50 per unit. This is based on long-term (after 2026) $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

That $5.50 per unit increase is due to several factors, each of which is relatively similar in terms of the impact on estimated value.

One factor is the much higher 2026 oil prices compared to when I looked at Dorchester in December 2025, leading to strong near-term distribution expectations. In December 2025, WTI oil strip prices were in the mid-to-high $50s. As well, the settlement payment should boost Dorchester's Q2 2026 distribution.

Another factor is my mid-single-digit upward revision in Dorchester's forward sales volumes, reflecting the impact of Dorchester's strong Q4 2025 sales volumes. I am still modeling Dorchester's forward sales volumes at several percent below Q4 2025 levels to be a bit conservative, though.

The final factor is a $5 increase (from $70 to $75) in my long-term oil price expectations, reflecting higher geopolitical risk going forward.

Conclusion

Dorchester Minerals announced a distribution above $0.75 per unit for Q4 2025, benefiting from strong oil sales volume growth and lease bonus income. Going forward, much improved oil prices may allow Dorchester's average quarterly distribution to reach close to $0.90 per unit during 2026, with a recent settlement adding over $0.30 per unit to its Q2 2026 distribution as well.

I've increased my estimate of Dorchester's value from $29 to $34.50 per unit due to a combination of strong oil sales volumes, excellent near-term oil prices, and a $5 per barrel increase in my long-term oil price.

data.title: Why Geopolitical Risk Is Fueling The Dollar's Surge
data.description:
By Erik Norland

With geopolitical risk in the forefront, many investors are asking a similar question: where should one turn for a safe haven? The Japanese yen, the Swiss franc, the U.S. dollar or gold? The reality is that there are no easy answers.

Since the Iran conflict began on February 28, the U.S. dollar has emerged as the primary flight-to-quality instrument, gaining around 3% while most foreign currencies fell against the greenback.

The Energy Advantage

In past crises, investors often fled to gold or the Japanese yen. Today, they're choosing the U.S. dollar – and energy is part of the reason why.

For decades, the yen was a reliable "flight to quality" instrument, but it has two disadvantages in the current environment. First, Japan produces very little of its own oil and natural gas.

Higher fuel prices mean higher import bills and slower growth, a dynamic that weighs directly on its currency. Second, Japan’s debt-to-GDP ratio stands at nearly twice that of the U.S. and six times that of Switzerland.

This debt burden didn’t matter when interest rates were stuck at zero. But now, with inflation above target and the Bank of Japan raising rates, long-term bond yields have surged. The soaring cost of financing that debt puts further downward pressure on the yen, altering the yen's role as a protective asset.

Conversely, the U.S. produces approximately 13.9 million barrels of crude oil daily, covering nearly all domestic demand. While energy-dependent regions like Europe, South Korea and Japan remain highly vulnerable to supply disruptions, America’s energy independence provides a distinct economic advantage. This asymmetry in energy exposure explains why the dollar is functioning as the preferred safe haven at the moment.

What About Gold?

Gold rallied nearly 100% in the year leading up to this conflict, meaning it entered the crisis already very expensive. Since then, it’s traded mostly sideways as many investors de-risk portfolios, selling equities and cryptocurrencies alongside precious metals.

Gold often anticipates inflation in advance. However, once that inflation actually arrives, the metal doesn’t always benefit immediately. Instead, rising inflation prompts central banks to raise interest rates or delay planned cuts, which makes fiat currencies relatively more attractive.

The Shift to Energy-Backed Currencies

With traditional safe havens showing mixed performance, where are investors turning? The focus seems to be shifting toward currencies of net energy exporters. For example, the Canadian dollar has strengthened against major currencies – Canada's oil production of approximately five million barrels per day, the vast majority destined for export markets, positions the loonie to benefit from elevated crude prices.

When it comes to an energy shortage crisis, which currency to select has become more important than ever.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Braze Remains Confident Of Revenue Growth Acceleration In The Coming Years, Maintain Buy
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Braze, Inc. (BRZE) previously with a buy rating, as I believed this stock should trade at a higher multiple given its growth potential and it being an AI beneficiary. For Q4 2026, bookings were very strong, enterprise traction continued well, and AI is now starting to show up in reported revenue. As such, I think BRZE deserves a higher valuation over time.

Q4 2026 earnings review

BRZE reported Q4 2026 revenue of $205.2 million, up 27.9% y/y from $160.4 million. Revenue included $5.7 million from BrazeAI Decisioning Studio, which implies 24.3% organic revenue growth for the quarter if we adjust for that. By segments, subscription revenue saw $193.5 million, while professional services and other revenue saw $11.7 million. Down the P&L, margins were lower y/y. Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 67.2%, down ~270 bps y/y, driven mainly by higher premium messaging volumes and hosting costs. That said, operating leverage was evident as non-GAAP EBIT grew to $14.5 million, almost double from $7.9 million last year, and non-GAAP EBIT margin expanded by 210 bps y/y to 7.1%.

Bookings and enterprise traction make the bull case stronger

There are plenty of data points from this past quarter that tell us growth is going to remain strong. I will start with bookings. Management said Q4 bookings were up a whopping >50% y/y and even said the average sales price reached a new high watermark with enterprise demand still especially strong. The pace of up-market penetration was also apparent as BRZE added 81 net new customers sequentially, and customers spending >$500k annually went up 35% y/y to 333. As of Q4 2026, those large customers now account for 64% of ARR, up 200 bps y/y. Also, BRZE ended Q4 with more than $1 billion of remaining performance obligations [RPO], up 30% y/y, with current RPO reaching $642 million, up 27% y/y. You put all these together, and the picture here is BRZE is not just growing; headline growth should see acceleration as the mix gets higher.

The other angle here is these large accounts appear to be using the platform in a much deeper way. Some data points cited by management include the following: among customers spending >$500k annually, >90% use Braze's SDK, >80% use Currents, and 50% already use Cloud Data Ingestion. While we don't know the revenue contribution, this is a strong testament to BRZE's upmarket push having substance. Customers are using the solutions, which makes BRZE even more embedded into how these customers collect data and run campaigns (i.e., it becomes harder to displace).

The AI aspect

Perhaps the biggest development to the equity story is AI impacts are starting to show up. For one, the BrazeAI Decisioning Studio contributed $5.7 million of revenue in the quarter. While that is small relative to the entire revenue base, it does provide real revenue evidence. With that, it provides the market with more confidence that BRZE's investments in AI solutions can ultimately be translated into revenue growth. This makes tracking the AI product roll-up/roadmap important, which has been progressing well. BRZE has launched both the Operator and Agent Console into general availability months ahead of schedule, and the early feedback was amazing. Operator was noted to already be used by more than two-thirds of customers after only a few weeks, while Agent Console adoption is growing on a week-on-week basis.

Importantly, management noted the operator is now accelerating and evolving workloads for thousands of BRZE dashboard users every week. This adoption momentum doesn't come from luck. It has to be the case that customers are finding value, and that means BRZE could monetize it eventually. If those are not enough to convince you BRZE is an AI beneficiary, consider that management said the AI roadmap helped with both win rates and deal velocity in Q4.

With that said, I would still caveat not to assume strong revenue contribution from AI in the near term, as management was clear that Agent Console revenue is recognized ratably over the life of the contract.

Valuation

The variant perception I have vs. the market is that I believe BRZE revenue growth will accelerate back to mid-20%, supported by growing enterprise penetration and more contribution from AI solutions. In my model, I expect BRZE to deliver ~$1.36 billion in revenues in FY2029, and the stock should be valued at a 6.5x forward revenue multiple if we compare it against its own trading history. Via this math, I think the stock price could roughly 3x from here, which is not really an impossible task since it once traded near $100. Note that the business is fundamentally stronger and margins are better today.

Risk

Q4 bookings and RPO were strong, but management also noted support from a large renewal quarter and a small increase in contract duration. This means that Q4 strength may not repeat, and if it slows down dramatically over multiple quarters, the market may not like it. Additionally, net retention is still below last year's levels. If renewals normalize and expansion stays muted, revenue growth could settle closer to 20% rather than reaccelerate.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on BRZE. The main reason is that the quarter strengthened my bull case that growth can move back toward the mid-20% range. Bookings, RPO, and large-customer growth all point to solid demand, while the AI roadmap has proven it can be translated into revenue. With that said, I would still watch the pace of near-term AI monetization and whether Q4 demand proves repeatable.

data.title: BGH: Reducing Duration Risk May Be Ideal For Today's Market Environment
data.description:
The Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with short-duration exposure to international high-yield debt issuances. With uncertainty growing in the US and European economies following the emergence of the Iranian war, I believe reducing duration risk may be prudent in order to optimize an investor’s fixed income exposure. While BGH can provide diversified exposure, investors may consider various ETFs as alternative investment strategies to capture similar high-yield exposure. Given the competitive costs and risks associated with the market environment, I am recommending BGH with a Hold rating.

Investment Thesis for Short-Duration High-Yield Fixed Income

The Iranian war has created substantial disruptions across the global economy, with energy prices exhibiting immediate effects that may ripple throughout other sectors in due time. Oil prices have stabilized above $100/bbl for both Brent crude and WTI and may remain elevated given the regional disruptions in the Middle East. According to the IEA, the Middle East provides roughly 30% of global oil and 17% of global natural gas production. With energy infrastructure and gas fields being critical targets of missile strikes resulting from the war, the global supply of oil and natural gas may not experience a recovery in the near future, resulting in higher prices for longer than expected.

The challenge is that there are no quick fixes to increasing production, let alone the transportation of commodities, relying on regions like Russia that had previously been embargoed due to the Ukrainian/Russian war.

While higher energy commodity prices are inflationary in nature, I believe the effects of higher energy prices have yet to trickle into inflationary data, potentially impacting other markets like consumer discretionary, transportation, petrochemicals, and other markets. Rationalizing this, higher gasoline and electricity prices may result in a reallocation of consumer spending and negatively impact consumer-facing markets. From a macroeconomic perspective, this could potentially result in higher inflationary pressures and weakening GDP growth.

This may place central banks in a peculiar position for 2026 as they weigh the impact of elevated pricing pressures on productivity. This could potentially result in a reversal of the rate-easing cycle experienced in recent years. If rates were to reverse course and begin climbing, long-duration bond strategies may suffer in price, whereas short-duration strategies may be relatively more insulated from changes in the key rate. This can be appealing for investors, as the pricing of the issuances held in the short-duration portfolio strategy may not be as significantly impacted by rate changes as those with longer-duration exposure. For reference, duration is the measure of price sensitivity relative to a change in the key rate.

As for the high-yield component of the portfolio, high-yield bonds typically trade directionally with the broader equity market, which may be the reason as to why BGH has faced a modest decline in share value in recent months. While rate policy may subtly influence the pricing of the portfolio, I believe broader market dynamics and economic expectations may be more heavily involved in the pricing of the strategy.

Specific to BGH, the fund’s top industries include health care & pharmaceuticals at 14.12%, energy at 10.39%, and telecommunications at 9.76%. The majority of bonds held in the portfolio are B-rated, making up 50% of the total assets. The strategy is primarily allocated to US-based issuances, making up 88.66% of assets. As of January 31, 2026, BGH was diversified across 174 individual issuances.

About Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund

BGH was launched by Barings on October 26, 2012, as a closed-end fund. The strategy has a management fee of 85 bps before applying leverage and other fees associated with the fund; the fund has a net expense ratio of 198bps.

BGH pays out a monthly distribution with a forward rate of $1.71/share, yielding 12.52%. Inclusive of the special dividend issued in August 2025, BGH paid out an annual dividend of $1.95/share over the last twelve months, yielding 14%.

BGH shares are currently trading at a -7% discount to NAV, its deepest discount since May 2025, presenting investors with an opportunity to purchase shares at a relatively appealing price.

As an alternative to BGH, investors that are more cost-sensitive may consider ETFs to gain high-yield exposure, including the iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG) and the Invesco Short Duration High Yield ETF (GTOH). For liquidity purposes, GTOH may be less ideal given that the fund only has $8.56 mm in net assets. While ETFs can provide high-yield, short-duration exposure, investors must weigh whether an active or passive management approach suits their investment needs. Given the rigid structure of GBH with respect to its allocation mandate, I believe ETFs can offer an appealing value. If the fund were to actively rotate duration in tandem with regional and sector exposure, I would argue that the managed strategy would be more ideal.

Risks Related to BGH

BGH is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with current income by allocating to short-duration, high-yield issuances, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making final investment decisions. High-yield bonds may exhibit greater credit risk to investors and may be significantly impacted by market dynamics as well as the risk of economic decline. High-yield bonds tend to trade directionally with the broader equity markets, potentially exposing investors to pricing risk if the equity market were to face further pressure. Geopolitical risks may influence the market, particularly as supply chains are disrupted as a result of the Iranian war. Investors will be exposed to manager risk, relying on the portfolio management team’s ability to appropriately select and weight securities.

Final Thoughts

Short-duration, high-yield fixed income can be an appealing market to be in during a time of economic uncertainty. While the broader market may face certain headwinds resulting from the supply chain disruptions in the Middle East, reducing duration risk may insulate investors from central bank rate policy if they were to reverse course in 2026 if inflationary pressures rise. Given the competitive dynamics offered by ETF strategies and the rigid allocation strategy of BGH, I am recommending the strategy with a Hold rating.
